_vars.raw_problem: Design a model evaluation strategy for a new credit risk prediction system to minimize overfitting, considering the available dataset consists of customer information and loan repayment history, and the model should be able to handle new, unseen data from different regions, the goal is to provide a reliable prediction of creditworthiness with a certain threshold of accuracy
_vars.student_framing: [{"section_type": "Problem Definition", "content": "Clearly define the objectives of the credit risk prediction system. Specify the target variable (e.g., loan repayment status: 'default' or 'no default') and the performance metric for evaluation (e.g., accuracy, F1 score, AUC-ROC)."}, {"section_type": "Data Description", "content": "Detail the dataset characteristics, including the number of features, types of customer information (e.g., income, credit history, employment status), and loan repayment history. Include information about the distribution of the data across different regions."}, {"section_type": "Model Selection", "content": "Identify potential machine learning models suitable for credit risk prediction (e.g., logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting). Consider the complexity of the models and their propensity to overfit."}, {"section_type": "Evaluation Strategy", "content": "Propose an evaluation strategy that includes cross-validation techniques (e.g., k-fold cross-validation) to ensure the model's robustness against overfitting. Discuss the importance of using a separate validation set and a test set."}, {"section_type": "Handling New Data", "content": "Outline methods for ensuring the model generalizes well to unseen data from different regions, such as domain adaptation techniques or stratified sampling to maintain regional representation in training."}, {"section_type": "Threshold Setting", "content": "Define how to determine an acceptable threshold for accuracy and other metrics, considering the business implications of false positives and false negatives in credit risk assessment."}]

criteria.name: Problem Definition
criteria.description: Student clearly defines the problem and identifies key elements of model evaluation and overfitting.
criteria.excellent_indicator: The problem definition is concise, well-structured, and directly related to model evaluation and overfitting.
criteria.poor_indicator: The problem definition is vague, incomplete, or unrelated to model evaluation and overfitting.

criteria.name: Relevant Concepts
criteria.description: Student identifies relevant concepts and techniques in model evaluation and overfitting, such as cross-validation and regularization.
criteria.excellent_indicator: The student identifies a range of relevant concepts and techniques, demonstrating a thorough understanding of model evaluation and overfitting.
criteria.poor_indicator: The student fails to identify key concepts and techniques or demonstrates a limited understanding of model evaluation and overfitting.

criteria.name: Evaluation Metrics
criteria.description: Student selects appropriate evaluation metrics for the problem, such as accuracy, precision, and recall.
criteria.excellent_indicator: The student selects a range of relevant evaluation metrics, demonstrating an understanding of their strengths and limitations.
criteria.poor_indicator: The student selects inappropriate or incomplete evaluation metrics, demonstrating a limited understanding of their use in model evaluation.

best_framing.base: Design a model evaluation strategy for a new credit risk prediction system to minimize overfitting, considering the available dataset consists of customer information and loan repayment history, and the model should be able to handle new, unseen data from different regions, the goal is to provide a reliable prediction of creditworthiness with a certain threshold of accuracy

entries.title: Definition of Creditworthiness
entries.description: For the purpose of this model, creditworthiness is defined as the likelihood of a customer repaying their loan within the agreed-upon timeframe, considering factors such as payment history, credit score, and income level.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem does not explicitly define what is meant by 'creditworthiness'.

entries.title: Threshold of Accuracy
entries.description: The certain threshold of accuracy is defined as achieving a minimum of 90% precision in predicting creditworthy customers, with a recall of at least 85% to ensure that most creditworthy customers are correctly identified.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem mentions a 'certain threshold of accuracy' without specifying what this threshold is.

entries.title: Class Imbalance
entries.description: The dataset is expected to be imbalanced, with a higher proportion of customers who have repaid their loans on time. The model should be designed to handle this imbalance, potentially through techniques such as oversampling the minority class, undersampling the majority class, or using class weights.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem does not mention how to handle potential class imbalance in the dataset.

entries.title: Data Quality
entries.description: The model should be robust to missing data and outliers. A data preprocessing step will be included to handle missing values and outliers, ensuring that the model is trained on high-quality data.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem assumes that the dataset is complete and of high quality without specifying how to handle missing data or outliers.

entries.title: Unseen Data
entries.description: When the problem mentions 'new, unseen data from different regions,' it refers to data from geographic locations not represented in the training dataset. The model should be able to generalize well to these new regions, taking into account potential differences in economic conditions, regulatory environments, and cultural factors.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem does not clarify what is meant by 'unseen data from different regions'.

entries.title: Model Performance Metrics
entries.description: The success of the model will be evaluated using a combination of metrics, including precision, recall, F1-score, and the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC). These metrics will provide a comprehensive view of the model's ability to predict creditworthiness accurately.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem mentions achieving a 'certain threshold of accuracy' but does not specify how accuracy will be measured.

entries.title: Model Complexity
entries.description: The model should balance complexity and interpretability. While deep learning models can offer high performance, the solution should also consider simpler models like logistic regression or decision trees if they can provide sufficient accuracy and are more interpretable.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem does not specify the preferred level of model complexity.

entries.title: Regulatory Considerations
entries.description: The model must comply with relevant financial regulations, such as anti-discrimination laws and data privacy regulations. The solution should ensure that the model does not inadvertently discriminate against certain groups of people and that all data handling practices are transparent and compliant with data protection laws.
entries.addresses_gap: The raw problem does not mention regulatory considerations that the model must adhere to.

best_framing.coverage_notes: This framing addresses all the criteria in the provided rubric, including problem definition, relevant concepts, and evaluation metrics. It also covers typical errors such as failure to consider class imbalance, inadequate handling of missing data, and insufficient consideration of model complexity. By including assumptions, constraints, clarifications, success metrics, scope boundaries, and domain context, this framing provides a comprehensive approach to designing a model evaluation strategy for the credit risk prediction system.

criticism: The student's framing response demonstrates a clear understanding of the problem and its key elements, such as defining the target variable and performance metrics for evaluation. However, in the 'Problem Definition' section, the student could have provided more specific details on how the objectives of the credit risk prediction system directly relate to minimizing overfitting. For instance, the student mentions ' Specify the target variable (e.g., loan repayment status: 'default' or 'no default') and the performance metric for evaluation (e.g., accuracy, F1 score, AUC-ROC)' but does not elaborate on how these specifications help in addressing overfitting. Furthermore, while the student identifies potential machine learning models and considers their complexity, the discussion on how these models' propensity to overfit will be addressed is somewhat superficial. The student should delve deeper into techniques such as regularization or early stopping to prevent overfitting in models like logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, and gradient boosting.
better_alternative: A stronger framing would include a more detailed explanation of how the selected performance metrics and machine learning models will be used to minimize overfitting. For example, the student could discuss how cross-validation techniques, such as k-fold cross-validation, will be employed to ensure the model's robustness against overfitting. Additionally, the student could provide more insight into how the model's ability to handle new, unseen data from different regions will be evaluated, potentially through the use of domain adaptation techniques or stratified sampling. By providing such details, the student can demonstrate a more comprehensive understanding of the problem and the strategies for addressing overfitting.
